subject: Chemistry
chapter: Classification of Elements and Periodicity in Properties
q:
In which of the following pairs, electron gain enthalpies of constituent elements are nearly the same or identical?

(A) Rb and Cs

(B) Na and K

(C) Ar and Kr

(D) I and At

Choose the correct answer from the options given below
question:
In which of the following pairs, electron gain enthalpies of constituent elements are nearly the same or identical?

(A) Rb and Cs

(B) Na and K

(C) Ar and Kr

(D) I and At

Choose the correct answer from the options given below
solution:
Rb & Cs have nearly same electron gain enthalpy Their values are nearly =-46 kJ/mol

Ar & Kr also have same ΔHe.g.. Value is +96 kJ/mol.

Na and K have different values (-53 and -48 kJ/mol) . And I and At has values of -295 and -270 kJ/mol. 

As we move down the group value of electron gain enthalpy decreases, with few exceptions.

question: The first and second ionisation enthalpies of a metal are <math><mn>496</mn></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>4560</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">k</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">J</mi><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">o</mi><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">l</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math>, respectively. How many moles of <math><mi mathvariant="normal">H</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">l</mi></math> and <math><msub><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">H</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></msub><mi mathvariant="normal">S</mi><msub><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">O</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>4</mn></mrow></msub><mo>,</mo></math> respectively, will be needed to react completely with <math><mn>1</mn></math> mole of the metal hydroxide?
solution:
Ionization Enthalpy values indicate that the metal belongs to Ist group, since the second ionization enthalpy is very high.
(Q - only one valence electron)
Metal hydroxide will be of the type, MOH.

MOH 1 mol + HCl 1 mol →MCl+ H 2 O

MOH+ 1 2 H 2 SO 4 → 1 2 M 2 SO 4 + H 2 O

(1mol) 1 2 mol So, one mole of HCl is required to react with one mole of MOH.

So, 1 2 mole of H 2 SO 4  is required to react with one mole of MOH.

Given below are two statements : Statement (I): According to the Law of Octaves, the elements were arranged in the increasing order of their atomic number.
Statement (II): Meyer observed a periodically repeated pattern upon plotting physical properties of certain elements against their respective atomic numbers. In the light of the above statements, choose the correct answer from the options given below :
question:

Statement (II): Meyer observed a periodically repeated pattern upon plotting physical properties of certain elements against their respective atomic numbers. In the light of the above statements, choose the correct answer from the options given below :
options: Both Statement (I) and Statement (II) are false.
Both Statement (I) and Statement (II) are true.
Statement (I) is false but Statement (II) is true.
Statement (I) is true but Statement (II) is false.
solution:
Law of octaves was arranged in the increasing order of their atomic weight.
Lothar Meyer plotted the physical properties such as atomic volume, melting point and boiling point against atomic weight.

q: The metal that has very low melting point and its periodic position is closer to a metalloid is
question: The metal that has very low melting point and its periodic position is closer to a metalloid is
solution:
The expected order of melting points of group 13 elements is B>Al>Ga>In>Tl.

But due to structural changes, melting point instead of decreasing, increases from Ga to Tl and Ga has the lowest melting point. Hence, the actual order is B>Al>Tl>In>Ga

Melting point

Al→933 K

Ga→303 K

In→430 K

Se→490 K

Ga has low meting point and it is closer to metalloid in periodic table.

solution:

subject: Chemistry
chapter: Classification of Elements and Periodicity in Properties
q: Metals generally melt at very high temperatures, Among the following which one has the highest melting point?
question: Metals generally melt at very high temperatures, Among the following which one has the highest melting point?
solution:
Transition metals have high melting point due to their high enthalpy of atomisation. They form strong metallic bonds with unpaired electrons.

Melting point of Ag=961.8°C

Melting point of Cs=28.5°C

Melting point of Ga=30°C

Melting point of Hg=-38.83°C

Cs,Ga and Hg are liquid at or near room temperature.

Among the given options Ag has the maximum Melting point, as it belongs to transition metal series.

subject: Chemistry
chapter: Classification of Elements and Periodicity in Properties
q:
Given below are two statements:
Statement - I: Along the period, the chemical reactivity of the element gradually increases from group 1 to group 18 .
Statement - II: The nature of oxides formed by group 1 element is basic while that of group 17 elements is acidic.
In the the light above statements, choose the most appropriate from the questions given below:
question:
Given below are two statements:
Statement - I: Along the period, the chemical reactivity of the element gradually increases from group 1 to group 18 .
Statement - II: The nature of oxides formed by group 1 element is basic while that of group 17 elements is acidic.
In the the light above statements, choose the most appropriate from the questions given below:
options: Both statement I and Statement II are true.
Statement I is true but Statement II is False.
Statement I is false but Statement II is true.
Both Statement I and Statement II is false.
solution:
The atomic and ionic radii, as we know, generally decrease in a period from left to right. As a consequence, the ionisation enthalpies generally increase (with some exceptions) and electron gain enthalpies become more negative across a period. In other words, the ionisation enthalpy of the extreme left element for a period is the least and the electron gain enthalpy of the element on the extreme right is the highest negative (Note: noble gases having completely filled shells have rather positive electron gain enthalpy values). This results in high chemical reactivity at the two extremes and the lowest in the centre. 


Group - 1 elements form basic nature oxides.  Metallic oxides are basic in nature because they react with dilute acids to form salt and water, while group - 17 elements form acidic oxides, An oxide that exhibits the property of acids is called acidic oxide. Since they are acidic, they react with a base to form a salt. Non-metals react with oxygen to form acidic compounds of oxides which are held together by covalent bonds. Therefore, statement - II is true.

q:
Given below are two statements. One is labelled as
Assertion A and the other is labelled as Reason R.
Assertion A : The first ionization enthalpy for oxygen is lower than that of nitrogen.

Reason R : The four electrons in 2p orbitals of oxygen experience more electron-electron repulsion.

In the light of the above statements, choose the correct answer from the options given below.
question:
Given below are two statements. One is labelled as
Assertion A and the other is labelled as Reason R.
Assertion A : The first ionization enthalpy for oxygen is lower than that of nitrogen.

Reason R : The four electrons in 2p orbitals of oxygen experience more electron-electron repulsion.

In the light of the above statements, choose the correct answer from the options given below.
options: Both <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">R</mi></math> are correct and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">R</mi></math> is the correct explanation of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi></math>.

Both <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">R</mi></math> are correct but <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">R</mi></math> is NOT the correct explanation of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi></math>.
A is correct but R is not correct.

<math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi></math> is not correct but <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">R</mi></math> is correct
solution:
Ionization energy of oxygen is less than that of nitrogen because 2p subshell of nitrogen is half filled, and half filled orbitals have some extra stability, so removing electrons from them will be little more difficult.
And In oxygen atom, 2 of the four 2p electrons must occupy the same 2p orbital resulting in an increased electron electron-repulsion.

N=1s22s22p3

O=1s22s22p4

question: The stability of dihalides of $\mathrm{Si}, \mathrm{Ge}, \mathrm{Sn}$ and $\mathrm{Pb}$ increases steadily in the sequence
solution:
Due to inert pair effect, the stability of $+2$ oxidation state increases as we move down this group. 
$\therefore \mathrm{SiX}_2 \ll \mathrm{GeX}_2 \ll \mathrm{SnX}{ }_2 \ll \mathrm{PbX}_2$
Hence, (C) is correct.

subject: Chemistry
chapter: Classification of Elements and Periodicity in Properties
q: The element having the highest first ionization enthalpy is
question: The element having the highest first ionization enthalpy is
solution:
The first ionisation energy is the energy needed to remove the outermost, or highest energy, electron from a neutral atom in the gas phase. 

The ionisation energy decreases from top to bottom in groups, and increases from left to right across a period. Nitrogen and carbon belong to second period elements, hence they have more ionisation energy than the other two elements.

It is highest for nitrogen because nitrogen is a more stable half-filled electronic configuration.

Al<Si<C<N; IE1 order.

Hence option C is the answer.

subject: Chemistry
chapter: Classification of Elements and Periodicity in Properties
q: In a period, the first ionisation enthalpy of the element at extreme left and the negative electron gain enthalpy of the extreme right element, except noble gases, are respectively.
question: In a period, the first ionisation enthalpy of the element at extreme left and the negative electron gain enthalpy of the extreme right element, except noble gases, are respectively.
solution:
In a period, as we move from left to right, the atomic size decreases and the effective nuclear charge increases.

Due to the large atomic size and low effective nuclear charge, the element at the extreme left (alkali metal) has the lowest first ionisation enthalpy in the period.

As we move to the extreme right (excluding noble gases), the elements (halogens) have the smallest atomic size and highest effective nuclear charge in the period. They require only one electron to complete their octet. Thus, they have the highest negative electron gain enthalpy.

Therefore, the first ionisation enthalpy of the extreme left element is the lowest, and the negative electron gain enthalpy of the extreme right element is the highest.

Answer: lowest and highest

q: Consider the elements $\mathrm{N}, \mathrm{P}, \mathrm{O}, \mathrm{S}, \mathrm{Cl}$ and F. The number of valence electrons present in the elements with most and least metallic character from the above list is respectively.
question: Consider the elements $\mathrm{N}, \mathrm{P}, \mathrm{O}, \mathrm{S}, \mathrm{Cl}$ and F. The number of valence electrons present in the elements with most and least metallic character from the above list is respectively.
solution:
Valence electrons: N(5), P(5), O(6), S(6), Cl(7), F(7).
Metallic character order in the list: P > S > N > Cl > O > F.
Most metallic character: P with 5 valence electrons.
Least metallic character (most nonmetallic): F with 7 valence electrons.
F is the most electronegative and has the smallest atomic size, making it most nonmetallic.

question:
The electron affinity value are negative for
A. \(\mathrm{Be} \rightarrow \mathrm{Be}^{-}\)
B. \(\mathrm{N} \rightarrow \mathrm{N}^{-}\)
C. \(\mathrm{O} \rightarrow \mathrm{O}^{2-}\)
D. \(\mathrm{Na} \rightarrow \mathrm{Na}^{-}\)
E. \(\mathrm{Al} \rightarrow \mathrm{Al}^{-}\)
Choose the most appropriate answer from the options given below :
options: A, B, D and E only
solution:
(A) $\mathrm{Be}+\mathrm{e}^{-} \rightarrow \mathrm{Be}^{-}, \quad$ E.A $=-$ ive
(B) $\mathrm{N}+\mathrm{e}^{-} \rightarrow \mathrm{N}^{-} \quad$ E.A $=$-ive
(C) $\mathrm{O}+\mathrm{e}^{-} \rightarrow \mathrm{O}^{-}$
$\mathrm{O}^{-}+\mathrm{e}^{-} \rightarrow \mathrm{O}^{-2} \quad$ E.A $=-$ ive
(D) $\mathrm{Na}+\mathrm{e}^{-} \rightarrow \mathrm{Na}^{-} \quad$ E.A $=+$ ive
(E) $\mathrm{A} \ell+\mathrm{e}^{-} \rightarrow \mathrm{A} \ell^{-} \quad$ E.A $=+$ ive

subject: Chemistry
chapter: Classification of Elements and Periodicity in Properties
q: First ionisation enthalpy values of first four group 15 elements are given below. Choose the correct value for the element that is a main component of apatite family :
question: First ionisation enthalpy values of first four group 15 elements are given below. Choose the correct value for the element that is a main component of apatite family :
solution:
The main component of apatite family is phosphorus.
Order of $\mathrm{IE}_1$ of group 15 elements $\mathrm{N}>\mathrm{P}>\mathrm{As}>\mathrm{Sb}$ IE of phosphorus $=1012 \mathrm{~kJ} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}$.

subject: Chemistry
chapter: Classification of Elements and Periodicity in Properties
q: The correct decreasing order for metallic character is
question: The correct decreasing order for metallic character is
solution:
A metal is a material that, when freshly prepared, polished, or fractured, shows a lustrous appearance, and conducts electricity and heat relatively well. Metals are typically malleable (they can be hammered into thin sheets) or ductile (can be drawn into wires)

On moving top to bottom of the periodic table metallic character increases and on moving left to right metallic character decreases.

Metallic character is directly proportional to the atomic size.

The correct order of atomic size of the given elements is Na>Mg>Be>Si>P.

Hence, correct order of metallic character is Na>Mg>Be>Si>P

All these elements are form 3rd period except beryllium which is from second period

Na is an alkali metal

Mg and Be are alkaline earth metals, 

Phosphorous belongs to a group which is commonly called as pnicogens.

Si belongs to 14th group

subject: Chemistry
chapter: Classification of Elements and Periodicity in Properties
q:
Consider the following elements.

Which of the following is/are true about A', B', C' and D' ?

A. Order of atomic radii: B'<A'<D'<C'

B. Order of metallic character : B'<A'<D'<C'

C. Size of the element : D'<C'<B'<A'

D. Order of ionic radii : B'+<A'+<D'+<C'+

Choose the correct answer from the options given below:
question:
Consider the following elements.

Which of the following is/are true about A', B', C' and D' ?

A. Order of atomic radii: B'<A'<D'<C'

B. Order of metallic character : B'<A'<D'<C'

C. Size of the element : D'<C'<B'<A'

D. Order of ionic radii : B'+<A'+<D'+<C'+

Choose the correct answer from the options given below:
solution:
In a group the atomic size increases due to the addition of shells as we move from one period to another. Across a period the atomic size decreases as the number of shells remain the same while the nuclear charge increases. This leads to the pulling of electrons from the outermost shell towards the nucleus thereby decreasing the size.

 Metallic character increases as we move down the group because the atomic size increases which lead to easy loss of electrons. On the other hand, it decreases across a period as we move from left to right. This happens because there is an increase in nuclear charge which makes it difficult for an atom to lose electrons.

In general along the period from left to right, size decreases and metallic character decreases.

In general down the group, size increases and metallic character increases.

B'<A' (size) C'>A' (size)

D'<C' (size) D'>B' (size)

B'<A' (metallic character)

D'<C' (metallic character)

B'+<A'+ (size)

D'+<C'+ (size)

∴ C statement is incorrect.

Hence ,option B is the answer.

subject: Chemistry
chapter: Classification of Elements and Periodicity in Properties
q: The element that does not show catenation is
question: The element that does not show catenation is
solution: Catenation is the tendency to link with one another through covalent bonds to form chain and rings, i.e., catenation. This is because bonds are very strong in the carbon family. On moving down the group, the size increases and the tendency to show catenation decreases because bond enthalpy decreases. The order of catenation is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi><mo>></mo><mi>Si</mi><mo>></mo><mi>Ge</mi><mo>></mo><mi>Pb</mi></math>. <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>Pb</mi></math> does not show catenation.
